title: 控方证人
summary:
During a dinner gathering, the psychiatrist Sir Arlington West discusses the nature of intuition and the subconscious with guests including Claire Trent, Jack Trent, and Dermot West. Sir Arlington explains that intuition is the subconscious mind processing subtle external cues that the conscious mind fails to register. Challenging Claire’s dismissal of premonition, Dermot describes his "red signals"—intuitive warnings of danger. He recounts an experience in the Mesopotamian plain where a feeling of danger prompted him to sleep outside his tent, saving him from an Arab servant who had slashed his bed. Dermot also mentions declining an invitation to a country house due to a "red alert," only for the house to be destroyed a week later.

As the conversation continues, Dermot experiences a sudden, intense "red signal" of danger. While he initially struggles to identify the source, noting his complicated romantic admiration for Claire and his friendship with Jack, he begins to scrutinize the group. He realizes he does not truly know the Trents and observes Sir Arlington watching Claire in an unsettling manner that leaves her appearing tense and afraid. Dermot also perceives that the dinner is a prelude to a séance, which Sir Arlington claims to support. Sir Arlington concludes the discussion by noting that individuals who suppress self-deception or hallucinations can become dangerous, harboring a "dangerous root" that threatens others while appearing normal.
